title: Axum
summary: HTTP routing and request-handling library focused on ergonomics and modularity, built in the Tokio ecosystem and commonly used with Tower and Hyper.
pros: good fit for Tokio-based stacks
clean handler and extractor model
easy integration with Tower middleware
predictable architecture for service-oriented backends
cons: less batteries-included than more opinionated frameworks
some ecosystem pieces require understanding Tower concepts
can feel lower-level than full-stack frameworks

title: Actix Web
summary: Powerful, pragmatic, and extremely fast web framework for Rust with strong routing, extractors, middleware, and HTTP/WebSocket support.
pros: rich built-in web features
strong extractor system
good performance reputation
mature ecosystem for backend work
cons: framework style is more opinionated than lower-level libraries
some APIs may feel less minimal than Axum
less aligned with Tower-first ecosystem design

title: Tokio
summary: Asynchronous runtime and platform for writing reliable Rust applications, providing async I/O, networking, scheduling, timers, and related building blocks.
pros: core foundation of modern async Rust backend development
large ecosystem and community adoption
good building blocks for networking applications
works well with Axum, Hyper, Tonic, Reqwest, and Tracing
cons: async Rust has a learning curve
runtime choices and task boundaries need discipline
debugging complex async flows can be harder than sync code

title: Tower
summary: Library of modular and reusable components for building robust clients and servers around the Service abstraction.
pros: excellent for composing backend concerns cleanly
fits naturally with Axum and Hyper stacks
encourages modular architecture
helps keep cross-cutting logic out of handlers
cons: Service abstraction takes time to internalize
can feel abstract for small projects
some middleware composition patterns are not beginner-friendly
source: Tower docs and Tokio ecosystem references

title: Hyper
summary: HTTP library for Rust focused on correctness, robustness, and performance, commonly used as a low-level building block for clients and servers.
pros: good low-level control
widely used foundation in Rust web stacks
strong fit for custom networking or protocol work
good choice when framework abstractions are too high-level
cons: lower-level than most application developers need
more boilerplate than Axum or Actix Web
less convenient for CRUD-style apps out of the box
source: hyper.rs guides and Hyper project site

title: Reqwest
summary: Ergonomic, batteries-included HTTP client for Rust, commonly used for consuming external APIs from async applications.
pros: easy to use for HTTP requests
good fit for agent tools and API integrations
typed JSON workflows are straightforward with Serde
works naturally in Tokio-based backends
cons: less control than lower-level HTTP libraries
TLS and feature choices matter in containerized environments
not the right tool if you need very low-level protocol handling

title: SQLx
summary: Async, pure Rust SQL toolkit with optional compile-time checked queries and support for PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, and SQLite without using a custom DSL.
pros: good choice when you want SQL instead of a heavy ORM abstraction
fits well with backend services and explicit query design
compile-time query checking is a strong feature
good interoperability with Axum and Tokio stacks
cons: more manual SQL than ORM-first approaches
schema evolution workflow needs discipline
compile-time checked mode adds setup complexity

title: tracing
summary: Framework for instrumenting Rust programs to collect structured, event-based diagnostic information, designed for application-level tracing.
pros: better fit than plain logging for async systems
structured events and spans are useful for backend observability
good integration across Tokio ecosystem libraries
valuable for debugging agent orchestration
cons: requires learning spans, events, and subscriber setup
needs consistent instrumentation to pay off
can add noise if log levels are not planned

title: Tonic
summary: Rust implementation of gRPC for building native client and server systems over HTTP/2, focused on interoperability and performance.
pros: strong choice for typed internal service communication
fits well with Tokio ecosystem
good when REST is not the best interface
pairs naturally with Protobuf-based contracts
cons: more setup and tooling than plain REST
not ideal for simple public APIs
introduces Protobuf and code generation workflow

title: Serde
summary: Framework for serializing and deserializing Rust data structures efficiently and generically across many data formats.
pros: essential part of most Rust backend stacks
excellent ergonomics for typed data models
works across JSON, config files, and other formats
reduces manual parsing boilerplate
cons: custom serialization cases can become verbose
derive-based models still need careful schema design
versioned API compatibility remains your responsibility

title: SeaORM
summary: Async and dynamic ORM for Rust with batteries-included features such as filters, pagination, and nested queries for REST, GraphQL, and gRPC APIs.
pros: good when you want ORM ergonomics instead of writing raw SQL everywhere
feature-rich for common application patterns
useful for faster CRUD-heavy backend work
covers many typical API-layer data access needs
cons: less explicit than direct SQL in complex cases
can hide query details behind ORM abstractions
not always the best fit for highly tuned database access paths
